front: Film: ends with the protagonist (David Hemmings) joining mimes in an imaginary tennis match in the park where he earlier photographed a murder
Film: a London fashion photographer realizes he has inadvertently photographed a murder after enlarging his park photos; based on Julio Cortazar's 'Las babas del diablo'
Film: Jeff Beck of The Yardbirds smashes his guitar at a concert; the protagonist grabs the guitar neck from the mob then discards it
Film: two nude women (including 19-year-old Jane Birkin and Gillian Hills) wrestle on an overturned clothes rack then on a large purple sheet of paper
Film: scored by Herbie Hancock — his first English-language film score; based on Julio Cortazar's story about a photographer named Roberto Michel
Film: the protagonist buys a large propeller for eight pounds from an antique shop with many statues, but later abandons it
Film: Anna disappears during a yacht trip to the Aeolian Islands; Claudia (Monica Vitti) and Sandro fall in love while searching for her, and the mystery is never resolved
Film: ends with Claudia stroking Sandro's weeping head on a bench as Mount Etna looms in the background
Film: booed at its 1960 Cannes premiere but won the Special Jury Prize 'for seeking to create a new film language'; placed 2nd in the 1962 Sight & Sound poll behind Citizen Kane
Film: Pauline Kael wrote about her year trying to 'persuade, goad, and even shame people into seeing' this film in her essay 'The Come-Dressed-As-the-Sick-Soul-of-Europe Parties'
Film: follows the dissolving marriage of Giovanni and Lidia over one day; ends with Marcello Mastroianni trying to make love to Jeanne Moreau in a golf course bunker
Film: Manny Farber criticized this film as 'an example of the evils of continuity' in his essay 'White Elephant Art vs. Termite Art'
Film: follows Vittoria, a translator, who begins an affair with Piero after meeting him at a stock exchange; ends with only shots of a streetlight where the couple agreed to meet
Film: Terence McNally called the ending 'the empty requiem for two dead souls' — it consists of shots of a streetlight where a man and woman agreed to meet
Film: famous for a seven-minute tracking shot that begins in a hotel room, moves out the window through the bars, circles the plaza, and returns to find hitmen have killed reporter David Locke
Film: Antonioni's only American film, included in The Fifty Worst Films of All Time; ends with Daria imagining a modernist house exploding in the desert in slow motion
Film: Pink Floyd's 'Us and Them' was based on music the band created for this film's score
Director: made the 'Trilogy of Alienation' — L'Avventura, La Notte, and L'Eclisse — all starring Monica Vitti; Seymour Chatman extends this to a tetralogy including Red Desert
Director: died on the same day as Ingmar Bergman, sparking critical debate about their respective cinematic styles
